contentSection.warning: Before making any modifications, it is highly recommended to back up your project. Use the Assembly Station demo scene first to verify the correct functioning of your modifications and to avoid creating errors in your main project.
contentSection.disclaimer: The images and videos in this documentation might not exactly match the current appearance of the asset, as Assembly Station is constantly evolving and being updated. I am committed to regularly updating this documentation and have avoided using overly technical language to make this documentation more accessible and easier to understand for everyone.

introduction.subtitle: Modular System to Bring Your Model to Life
introduction.heroText: Assembly Station is the ultimate tool for creating interactive assembly instructions in Unity. Designed to streamline complex assembly processes with intuitive visual feedback.
introduction.getBtnAria: Get Assembly Station from Unity Asset Store
introduction.getBtnTitle: Purchase Assembly Station on Unity Asset Store
introduction.docSubtitle: Your complete resource for mastering Assembly Station, a powerful tool to create interactive assembly instructions in Unity.
introduction.aboutP1: Assembly Station is a crafting and assembly system designed to optimize production processes in Unity projects. Whether you are building complex machinery, creating unique items, or improving workflows, Assembly Station offers intuitive tools to assemble components efficiently and precisely.
introduction.aboutP2: This documentation site provides comprehensive guides and resources to help you get started with Assembly Station and make the most out of its features.
introduction.docCardText: Detailed guides and tutorials for your Unity projects
introduction.tutCardText: Step-by-step videos to master advanced features
introduction.verCardText: Track changes and improvements over time
introduction.ctaText: Get your copy of Assembly Station and start creating interactive assemblies today

contacts.title: Contact Me
contacts.subtitle: I'm here to help! Get in touch with me.
contacts.infoText: Don't hesitate to contact me for any questions, feedback, or assistance requests.
contacts.discordVerificationText: After purchasing the asset, please send me your order number via Discord private message to get verified.
contacts.supportText: If you find my work helpful, consider supporting me to help maintain and develop new features.
contacts.honeypot: Don't fill this out if you're human:

reportBug.title: Report a Bug
reportBug.subtitle: Have you found a bug or have a proposal to improve Assembly Station? Let me know so I can fix it!
reportBug.trackingText: All reported issues are tracked and prioritized based on severity and impact.
reportBug.responseText: I'm currently the only developer, so I'll do my best to resolve issues as quickly as possible.
reportBug.privacyText: Your information is kept confidential and only used for resolving the reported issue.
reportBug.recommendedText: Using GitHub allows you to easily track the status of your bug report and follow updates as I work on fixes.
reportBug.githubText: Create an issue on my GitHub repository (recommended)
reportBug.emailText: Send me an email with details
reportBug.verificationText: After purchasing the asset, please send me your order number via Discord private message to get verified.
reportBug.formSubtitle: If you prefer not to use GitHub, you can fill out this form to report an issue directly.
reportBug.severityCritical: Critical - App crashes or cannot function
reportBug.severityMinor: Minor - Non-critical issue or UI problem
reportBug.stepsPlaceholder: 1. Open scene... 2. Select object... 3. Click on...
reportBug.screenshotsHelp: Attach any relevant screenshots or videos (optional)
reportBug.faq1Question: What happens after I submit a bug report?
reportBug.faq1Answer: After submitting a bug report, I will review the issue and respond as soon as possible. I'll provide updates on the status of the fix and may request additional information if needed. Once fixed, you'll receive a notification and the fix will be included in the next update.
reportBug.faq2Question: How can I check the status of my bug report?
reportBug.faq2Answer: You can check the status of your report through the email you provided in the report. If you reported via GitHub, you can track the issue there. I also send email updates when there's progress on your reported issue.
reportBug.faq3Question: What information is most helpful in bug reports?
reportBug.faq3List1: Clear, detailed steps to reproduce the issue
reportBug.faq3List2: Screenshots or screen recordings showing the problem
reportBug.faq3List3: Exact version information of Assembly Station and Unity
reportBug.faq3List5: When the issue started occurring (after an update, etc.)

notFound.subtitle: Page Not Found
notFound.text: Sorry, the page you are looking for does not exist or has been moved.
notFound.seoDescription: The page you are looking for does not exist. Return to Assembly Station homepage or explore our documentation.

tutorials.title: Video Tutorials
tutorials.lead: Learn how to use Assembly Station with these step-by-step video tutorials that cover everything from creating prefabs to managing resources.
tutorials.youtubeLead: You can also {0} on my YouTube channel.
tutorials.videoNotSupported: Your browser does not support HTML5 video.
tutorials.t1Desc: In this video, you'll learn how to create a prefab, set the pivot correctly, add materials, descriptions, names, and other essential properties.
tutorials.t1Caption: Tutorial: Creating and Setting Up Prefabs
tutorials.t2Desc: This video demonstrates how to create and manage category buttons for each prefab to organize your assembly system.
tutorials.t2Caption: Tutorial: Creating Category Buttons for Prefabs
tutorials.t3Desc: In this tutorial, we'll cover how to create and manage tags for each component to enhance organization and filtering.
tutorials.t3Caption: Tutorial: Creating and Managing Tags for Components
tutorials.t4Desc: This video demonstrates how the Assembly Station system works and how users can interact with it to build their models.
tutorials.t5Desc: Learn how to manage and create groups for components, allowing for better organization and more complex structures. You can enter them in 2 ways: Individually for each piece or via the group management system.
tutorials.t6Desc: In this final tutorial, we'll cover how to manage resources for each component in your Assembly Station project.
tutorials.needHelp: Need more help? Check out these resources:

versions.title: Released Versions
versions.lead: Track the evolution of Assembly Station with our version history. Click on a version for details or download directly from Unity Asset Store.
versions.seoTitle: Version History - Assembly Station Unity Asset
versions.seoDescription: Complete version history and changelog for Assembly Station Unity asset. See all updates, new features, bug fixes and improvements.
versions.seoKeywords: Assembly Station versions, changelog, update history, Unity asset updates, version history
versions.v155c1: Fixed and improved the Demo Scene experience.
versions.v155c2: Improved the player view in the Demo Scene for better clarity and usability. Additional fixes and adjustments.
versions.v155c3: Improved UI adaptation across different screen sizes and resolutions to ensure better compatibility on all displays.
versions.v154c2: Fixed an issue where changing the Orbit Camera zoom sensitivity was not applied correctly.
versions.v154c3: Fixed a bug that allowed camera rotation and 3D model interaction while the mouse was over the UI.
versions.v154c4: Fixed an issue that prevented the assigned group name from being displayed correctly on a piece.
versions.v154c6: Added a new resource editing system:
versions.v154c7: Users can now freely add new resources.
versions.v154c8: Resources are dynamically updated in the visual/UI layer.
versions.v154c9: Improved the resource cost assignment workflow for pieces:
versions.v154c10: Existing resources can be selected directly from the Resource Layer.
versions.v154c11: New resources can be added during the assignment process.
versions.v154c12: Overall workflow is faster, clearer, and more practical.
versions.v143c2: Improved Camera Controls: Camera rotation is now smoother and no longer locks up when the cursor passes over UI elements.
versions.v143c3: System Optimizations: Significantly improved loading speeds and reduced memory consumption during extended play sessions.
versions.v143c4: Smart Group Building: When building multiple pieces together, resources are verified and consumed only if sufficient for the entire group, preventing partial builds.
versions.v143c6: Fixed the "Save" button remaining disabled after unbuilding pieces.
versions.v143c7: Fixed visual issues with materials on objects with multiple LODs.
versions.v143c8: Improved resource handling for more consistent behavior.
versions.v143c9: Fixed several minor bugs that could cause slowdowns or freezes in specific situations.
versions.v133c2: You can now lock pieces during the building process! To unlock them, you'll need to build a required number first — introducing a new progression system that adds strategic depth and planning to construction.
versions.v133c3: Fixed an issue where the squares in the left panel (representing each piece) didn't wrap correctly.
versions.v133c4: Fixed a bug where long descriptions would not wrap properly, causing layout and UI issues.
versions.v123c1: It is now possible to safely move the assembly point without manually realigning it to the boundaries or the grid
versions.v122c1: Small improvements to the category panel for better usability
versions.v122c2: Refined click behavior on piece buttons for a smoother and more consistent interaction experience
versions.v121c1: Object placement grid added for easier and more precise positioning
versions.v121c2: Improved camera control: each piece can now have a custom zoom distance and angle when focusing
versions.v121c3: Added custom orbit angles per prefab for more tailored camera behavior
versions.v121c4: Fixed UI behavior issues related to piece selection and confirmation
versions.v111c1: Full support for models with LOD (Level of Detail)
versions.v111c2: Ability to load more complex and detailed models
versions.v111c3: Improved camera control: each piece can now have a custom zoom distance and angle when focusing on it
versions.v111c4: Fixed an issue where the button associated with a piece would not behave correctly after confirmation

beta.title: Upcoming Features
beta.lead: Get a sneak peek at features I'm currently developing for Assembly Station.
beta.feature1Desc: My new positioning system gives you complete control over each prefab element before assembly. You can precisely position components to create more realistic and intuitive assembly instructions.
beta.step1: Select the prefab component you want to reposition
beta.step2: Enter positioning mode through the component inspector
beta.step3: Use the transformation handles to adjust position and rotation
beta.step4: Save your custom positioning for future assemblies
beta.benefit1: Precise control over each component's position in your assemblies
beta.benefit2: Real-time visual feedback as you position elements
beta.benefit3: Create more intuitive assembly sequences with proper spatial relationships
beta.benefit4: Simplifies the workflow for complex multi-part assemblies
beta.benefit5: Seamlessly integrates with existing prefab structures
beta.feedbackText: Have suggestions for what you'd like to see in Assembly Station?

pricing.title: Pricing
pricing.lead: One single price, complete access: everything you need to excel in assembly guides
pricing.feature1: Complete Unity Asset for Assembly Instructions
pricing.faq1Q: Is this a one-time payment or subscription?
pricing.faq1A: This is a one-time purchase. You'll get the current version and all future updates to the asset with no additional costs.
pricing.faq2Q: Can I use this asset in multiple projects?
pricing.faq2A: Yes, once purchased, you can use Assembly Station in multiple projects according to Unity Asset Store's standard license terms.
pricing.faq3Q: How do I get support if I have questions?
pricing.faq3A: You can contact me directly through the support channels provided in this documentation. I'm committed to helping you implement Assembly Station successfully in your projects.
pricing.moreInfo: Need more information before making a decision?

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.lead: Welcome to Assembly Station's privacy policy. This policy will help you understand what data we collect, why we collect it, and what your rights are regarding it.
privacy.itLead: La tua privacy è importante per noi. Questa pagina spiega le nostre pratiche sulla privacy.
privacy.itDesc1: Questa Informativa sulla Privacy descrive come raccogliamo, utilizziamo e proteggiamo le tue informazioni personali. Ci impegniamo a garantire la sicurezza e la riservatezza dei tuoi dati.
privacy.itDesc2: Quando utilizzi il nostro sito web o i nostri servizi, potrebbero essere raccolte alcune informazioni per migliorare la tua esperienza e aiutarci a comprendere meglio come gli utenti interagiscono con la nostra piattaforma. Raccogliamo solo i dati necessari e li utilizziamo nei modi descritti in questa informativa.

cookiePolicy.title: Cookie Policy
cookiePolicy.lead: Welcome to Assembly Station's cookie policy. This policy will help you understand what cookies and tracking technologies we use, how we use them, and what your rights are regarding them.
cookiePolicy.itLead: Informazioni sui cookie utilizzati sul sito web di Assembly Station.
cookiePolicy.itDesc1: Questa pagina fornisce informazioni sui cookie utilizzati sul nostro sito web. I cookie sono piccoli file di testo che vengono memorizzati sul tuo computer o dispositivo mobile quando visiti il nostro sito web.
cookiePolicy.itDesc2: Utilizziamo i cookie per migliorare la tua esperienza di navigazione, analizzare il traffico del sito e personalizzare i contenuti. Utilizzando il nostro sito web, acconsenti all'uso dei cookie in conformità con questa Politica dei Cookie.

installation.title: 1. GET START
installation.lead: The project internally uses Text Mesh Pro and Input System. Please follow the steps below for proper installation and setup.
installation.section1Text: The TextMesh Pro package is included in Unity Editor. It is not necessary to install it separately. You can follow the simple guide at the link to insert it into your project
installation.section1NoteText: There may be an issue where you do not see text on buttons or other UI elements. This occurs because the text components don't have an assigned font. Simply select the text component and assign a base or custom font to resolve this issue.
installation.section2Text: At the beginning of the project, there is a basic player that can move freely and access the Assembly Station via a button. The player movement is controlled by the
installation.section2TextEnd: , and it contains a script that manages the Assembly Station resources.
installation.section2Option1: If your project already has a player, you can use your own Input System and add the RESOURCES COMPONENT PLAYER script to your player components.
installation.section2Option2: If you want to use the included player, you'll need to install the INPUT SYSTEM package from the Unity Package Manager.
installation.section2Option3: If your project already has an Input System implementation, simply modify the PLAYER CONTROLLER script to integrate with your existing system.

fbxGuide.title: 2. FBX AND MATERIALS GUIDE
fbxGuide.lead: Pay attention to this part, it is essential for using the asset. Assembly Station works through SKINNED MESH RENDER and MESH RENDER.
fbxGuide.section1Text: If you have an FBX model, you need to extract all the materials and put them in the Resources folder within the path Assets > AssemblyStation > Resources.
fbxGuide.section2Text1: To extract the materials, navigate to the following location:
fbxGuide.section2Text2: And select the Resources folder as shown in the images on the right.
fbxGuide.section3Text1: Once done, go back to the FBX model and select the options indicated by the arrows in the image on the right.
fbxGuide.section3Text2: Automatically the model will recognize the materials present in the folder.
fbxGuide.section3Text3: For better organization I recommend creating a folder for each model and putting the materials inside it. as in the example on the side.

prefabs.title: 3. PLACE PREFABS
prefabs.lead: Once the materials are separated, navigate to the Prefab Clone folder: Assets > AssemblyStation > Prefab Clone.
prefabs.rotationText: It's important to consider the rotation with which the prefab will be instantiated in the Assembly Station. This rotation determines the initial orientation of the model when it's displayed.
prefabs.rotationImgCaption: Managing the prefab rotation during instantiation
prefabs.assemblyPointText: The Assembly Station places the prefab at a specific point called Assembly Point. This is a key reference for positioning your models.
prefabs.pivotPointText: That point refers to the parent pivot, which contains all the Elements that make up the gameobject. Positioning this pivot correctly is critical to the correct positioning of the model.
prefabs.placementText: When we place the prefab, the pivot point corresponds to the Assembly Point. To ensure that the prefab does not float or intersect with the ground, you need to move the parent's pivot. (Starting from the FBX)
prefabs.unpackText: In this case, as you can see, the pivot is slightly shifted upwards. To adjust it, first press Unpack Completely to break the prefab connection.
prefabs.adjustStep1: Select everything except the parent (in this case Mech Rework) and move everything out of the parent.
prefabs.adjustStep2: Select the parent (Mech Rework) and move the pivot.
prefabs.adjustStep3: To move the pivot, just click on the green arrow and move it downwards. I recommend activating the 2D view for a better view.
prefabs.adjustStep4: As you can see, the model will not move in space but only the pivot.
prefabs.adjustImg1Caption: Moving child objects out of the parent
prefabs.groundText: In this case, the Mecha is aligned at the bottom to make it appear resting on the ground. So I moved the pivot slightly downwards. This depends on you based on how you want to display the model.
prefabs.airText: You can also position the model in the air (for example, if it is a weapon). In this case, the pivot is very low, so the model remains suspended.
prefabs.createText: Once done, select all the children and put them back inside the parent as before. Then, drag the parent into the (Prefab clone) folder and a Prefab will be created automatically.
prefabs.cameraText1: Once in game, the camera that rotates around the placed prefab will have as a reference point the Orbit Camera Point that will always be added automatically with Add Name and Descriptions To Prefab.
prefabs.cameraWarning: IMPORTANT: Before modifying the OrbitCameraPoint, you must go to documentation point 4 and execute the "Add Name and Descriptions To Prefab" step. This will ensure the Orbit Camera Point is properly added to your prefab.
prefabs.cameraText2: In Editor mode, you can move the pivot point of ObitalCameraPoint to make the camera lower or higher as needed.

descriptions.title: 4. ADD DESCRIPTIONS AND IMAGES TO PREFABS
descriptions.lead: Once you have added all the desired prefabs and moved the pivots, you need to add a description and an image to each child that contains MeshRenderer or SkinnedMeshRenderer, while the parent needs to be assigned a title.
descriptions.leadAuto: All this is done <u>automatically</u> by the asset (depending on the number of prefabs in the folder, it will take more or less time).
descriptions.autoText: Just click on the button (at the top) Tools > Add Name and Descriptions To Prefab, select the prefab clone folder, and a description, name, and image will be automatically assigned to each child and a title to the parent.
descriptions.autoWarningText: It is recommended to hide all objects in the scene before performing this operation, otherwise the screenshot will not be processed correctly. Other objects in the scene might appear in the automatic screenshots and compromise the quality of the generated images.
descriptions.fieldsText: Do not insert anything inside the field
descriptions.accessText: Double-click with the mouse on Piece Description or Preview Description to go directly to the corresponding piece in the folder Assets > AssemblyStation > ScriptableObjects
descriptions.editTitleText1: A window will open with all the scriptable objects created for each Prefab, here you can enter the name of the prefab and the description.
descriptions.editTitleText2: You can also adjust the camera angle, distance, and rotation to customize how you want to see the selected prefab when it's focused. These settings will be used when the component is highlighted in the interactive view.
descriptions.editPieceText: While if you select Piece Description, a window will open with all the scriptable objects created for each child of the prefab, here you can enter the name, description, image, and other things that we will see later.
descriptions.modelPreviewText: This is what will be displayed in the game for the model Preview.
descriptions.piecePreviewText: This is what will be displayed in the game for the piece Preview.
descriptions.imageManageText1: Each "piece" will have an associated image that will be displayed in the preview.
descriptions.imageManageText2: The image is generated automatically when we press the button Tools > Add Name and Descriptions To Prefab
descriptions.imageManageText3: If you want to insert a custom image, just go to the Piece Description and select the desired image, replacing the one generated by the system.
descriptions.imageManageText4: Functionality (Editor Mode): when clicking on Add Name and Descriptions To Prefab, a camera is generated in front of the piece and a photo is taken, this photo is saved in the folder Assets > AssemblyStation > Screenshot > and is associated with the child. For obvious reasons, the image may be compromised by the size of the piece and an incorrect angle. For this reason, it is recommended to insert a custom image if this problem occurs.

buttons.title: 5. BUTTONS AND CATEGORIES FOR PREFABS
buttons.heroText: To insert the buttons, just go to the Hierarchy and select Content UI
buttons.accessText: Content UI is the component that manages the user interface of the Assembly Station. By selecting it, you can access the button and category settings.
buttons.configText: In the inspector, a section will appear where you can insert the buttons and categories for the prefabs. This mechanic is used to add various categories so that in the game the user can select the various models more easily.
buttons.configKeyFunc: Key functionality: This function adds the buttons in Editor mode, spacing them automatically between them for optimal interface arrangement.
buttons.attentionText: To better understand the functionality, we will follow a detailed video tutorial.
buttons.attentionBanner: PAY CLOSE ATTENTION TO THIS PART
buttons.keyInitText: Initially, the categories will be 2: Cube, Cylinder
buttons.keyOrderText: It is <u>Fundamental</u> to follow the order of the various lists as shown in the video
buttons.keyNumberText: The new buttons you will insert will be numbered in the list, so the models corresponding to the buttons must be in the same position in the Button list
buttons.addBtnTitle: ADDING A BUTTON TO AN EXISTING CATEGORY
buttons.addBtnHow: How to add a button to a previously created category:
buttons.addBtnNote: (in your version not all prefabs are present as in the video, but it serves to make you understand that you will have to use the same procedure with your prefabs)
buttons.addBtnWarning: PRECISELY ASSIGN ALL CATEGORIES, BUTTONS, AND PREFABS AS IN THE VIDEO OTHERWISE IT WILL NOT WORK
buttons.addBtnFollow: If you want to add a button to a previously created category, just follow the video tutorial below:
buttons.addBtnCaption1: Tutorial: Adding a button to an existing category
buttons.addBtnCaption2: Tutorial: Complete procedure for adding new buttons
buttons.newCatText1: If you want to add a new category in addition to the first 3, follow the video tutorial below.
buttons.newCatText2: To add a 4th category, as you will see in the video, I had to first add and assign the Tools category that in the previous video was not inserted in the list because there were no buttons assigned to a prefab.
buttons.newCatText3: This is because, as explained, it is fundamental to keep the lists correctly numbered otherwise the system gives an error. It doesn't make sense to leave an empty category, but I wanted to show you this example for completeness.
buttons.newCatTip: Tip: As you will see in the video, I duplicated the button to then change the text. This is because each button has a script and an assigned number fundamental for the functionality. When adding a new category, duplicate an existing button, change the text and update the number that will progress with the addition of categories.
buttons.newCatCaption: Tutorial: Procedure for adding a new category
buttons.activationRule:
Every time a category is added, it must be disabled
Only the first one should be left active
buttons.videoFallback: Your browser does not support HTML5 video.

categories.title: 6. TAGS/CATEGORIES/GROUPS OF PIECES
categories.lead: Once all the texts and images have been inserted, you can proceed with creating the TAGS and GROUPS.
categories.noCatText: Initially, each piece of the prefab will not have a tag and will therefore be visible in the panel in a single list.
categories.assignText: If you want to divide the pieces by category, just create a tag and assign it to each piece for each category. You can select multiple tags at the same time by holding down the CTRL key and selecting the various Pieces, otherwise by pressing the CTRL+SHIFT key.
categories.resultText: By doing so, the pieces will be divided based on the tag you assigned. The panel will show pieces organized by categories.
categories.groupsText:
Each piece that contains a SkinnedMeshRenderer or a MeshRenderer will be displayed in the panel. This means that, in the presence of models with hundreds of pieces, the resulting list will be very long, making it difficult for the user to quickly find the desired piece.

To solve this problem, I introduced Groups, which allow you to organize the pieces into specific categories, simplifying the search and management. To better clarify the concept, here is an example:
categories.comparisonTitle: COMPARISON: PIECES WITH AND WITHOUT GROUPS
categories.withoutGroupsText: In this case, the pieces do not have an assigned group and will therefore be displayed as 6 separate buttons.
categories.withGroupsText1: Once the group is assigned, here is the result: 1 button for 6 pieces.
categories.withGroupsText2: <u>Any number of pieces can be combined into a group.</u>
categories.videoText: To assign the group, follow the video tutorial below that shows the complete process.
categories.videoPath: GroupPieces folder within the path Assets > AssemblyStation > ScriptableObjects > GroupPieces.
categories.videoFallback: Your browser does not support HTML5 video.
categories.autoSelectText: When using Groups, the image is automatically selected from the first piece of the group, as well as the description and name.
categories.autoSelectDetail: To select the first item on the list, simply check the checkbox next to the name. It will be assigned as the first, and all resources, names, images, and camera zoom will be linked to it. Therefore, it is necessary to assign all these elements to that piece.
categories.namingWarning1: Pay close attention to group organization! Do not add pieces from different prefabs to the same group. Instead, create separate groups for each prefab.
categories.namingWarning2: Additionally: All pieces assigned to the same group should have the same tag. Mixing different tags within the same group can cause issues.
categories.namingExampleText: If you mix pieces from different prefabs in the same group, the system will not function correctly. Always create distinct groups for each prefab, even if the pieces have similar functions or attributes.
categories.lodText: When working with LOD (Level of Detail) Groups, the same grouping and tagging principles apply. For consistency and proper functionality, follow these guidelines:
categories.lodRule1: Apply the same tag to all LOD levels of the same model to maintain consistency
categories.lodRule2: When adding pieces to a group, make sure to include all relevant LOD levels with the same tag
categories.lodRule3: Each LOD level should be properly tagged to ensure that the UI system can correctly identify and group them
categories.lodRule4: Remember: Even though only the first piece with the Descriptor Component will be considered for display in the UI, applying the same tag to all LOD levels is essential for visual coherence and proper system functionality
categories.lodNoteText: When grouping LOD levels, apply the same tag to all levels (LOD0, LOD1, LOD2, etc.) of the model you want to group together. Even though the system will only use the Descriptor Component from the first piece for displaying information, maintaining consistent tagging across all LOD levels ensures proper grouping behavior and visual coherence in the interface.

resources.title: 7. RESOURCES
resources.videoText: Tutorial on how to set up resources for each prefab piece: assign which resources are used, define the cost for every piece, and create new resources.
resources.videoCaption: Tutorial: how to configure resources, costs and new entries
resources.videoFallback: Your browser does not support HTML5 video.
resources.infoBox: By default, the Assembly Station uses a resource system, where each piece has a cost that is deducted from the player's inventory.
resources.playerText: The player's resources are located in the folder: Assets > AssemblyStation > ScriptableObject > PlayerResources . This is assigned to the test Player through the script (ResourcesComponentPLayer) that you can find in PLayer Test in the Hierarchy
resources.manageText: In this section you will assign the quantity of resources available to the player; if needed, you can also add new ones (if you haven't already), as shown in the video above.
resources.costText1: As seen in chapter 4 this is the section where you can add the various texts, images, etc. in the single piece. As you can see, there are 2 sections we haven't discussed yet: RESOURCES COSTS and SHOW IN UI.
resources.costText2: Here you can enter the costs for each piece and decide whether to make the resources visible or not in the interface.
resources.panelText1: The resources panel is automatically updated with the resources assigned to the player.
resources.panelText2: In the Hierarchy, simply select Panel_Resource as shown in the image and, in the Panel Resource (Script) section, drag the resource text and assign the one you want.
resources.panelText3: If you want to add a new resource, just copy the existing parent/child structure of the current resources and configure it following the same pattern.
resources.alertText1: For the pieces that have a Group, the same rule always applies: the first in the list is taken. So the resources will need to be changed in that specific piece.
resources.alertText2: In the case of pieces with a Group, remember that the resources must always be changed in the first piece of the Group, as the data related to image, description, and cost are taken from there.

effects.title: 8. SOUND & VISUAL EFFECTS
effects.lead: When building a component in Assembly Station, custom sound effects and visual particles play to enhance the user experience. Each model can have its own characteristic sound and visual effect.
effects.howText: Every time a piece is built, a sound will play along with a visual effect. To ensure that each model has its own characteristic sound and effect, the system uses two separate lists:
effects.howList1: Sound Effects List: Contains all the audio clips that play when building models
effects.howList2: Visual Effects List: Contains all the particle effects that display when building models
effects.howNoteText: The order of effects in both lists must match the order of prefabs in the Construction UI. This ensures the correct sound and effect are played for each model.
effects.matchText: For proper configuration, you need to ensure that the order of elements in your effects lists matches the order in the Construction UI prefabs list. For example:
effects.matchAssignTitle: The equivalent effect assignments would be:
effects.matchNote: This one-to-one mapping ensures that when a user selects and builds a specific model, the correct sound and particle effect will be triggered, enhancing the immersive experience.
effects.placementText1: The system also includes sound effects for the final prefab placement. When a user completes the assembly process and places the final model in the environment, a dedicated sound effect plays to provide satisfying feedback.
effects.placementText2: These placement effects help create a more immersive experience and provide audio confirmation that the placement action has been completed successfully.

saves.title: 9. SAVES
saves.saveText: Once a piece has been assembled, the Save Build button will become active. Clicking it will save the model's progress.
saves.filesText1: Model saves are located in the folder: Assets > AssemblyStation > Saves.
saves.filesText2: The save files are divided into two parts:
saves.filesList1: A JSON file that contains the model with a unique ID.
saves.filesList2: A file for saving each individual assembled piece.
saves.filesList3: If, after saving and returning to Editor mode, the JSON files do not appear, open the scripting editor, make a small change (such as adding a space or a tab), then return to Editor. The files will be generated.
saves.filesReset: To reset a model, you can either delete both files or open them and manually remove the specific pieces you want to restore.
saves.howText: When you click the Save Build button, the system records the following information:
saves.howList2: Which specific pieces have been placed
saves.howResult: The next time the player opens the Assembly Station and selects the same model, all previously assembled pieces will be automatically displayed as completed.

knownIssues.title: 10. KNOWN ISSUES
knownIssues.errorsText: It can happen that, when you go to build certain pieces, this type of error appears:
knownIssues.troubleText1: This may happen because the component might not have the PieceDescription assigned. Simply locate the component and assign it correctly.
knownIssues.troubleText2: The video below shows how to fix this issue:
knownIssues.troubleCaption: Tutorial: How to fix naming conflicts
knownIssues.videoFallback: Your browser does not support HTML5 video.
knownIssues.tipsList1: Ensure all materials are correctly placed in the Resources folder
knownIssues.tipsList2: Check that prefabs are properly set up with appropriate pivots
knownIssues.tipsList3: Verify that all required components are assigned in the inspector
knownIssues.tipsList4: Make sure there are no duplicate names in your project pieces
knownIssues.tipsList5: Check for error messages in the Unity console
knownIssues.helpText: If you're experiencing issues not covered in this documentation, feel free to contact me for support.

settings.title: 13. GENERAL SETTINGS
settings.descSpriteButton: The sprite used for the build button.
settings.descUnbuildButtonSprite: Sprite to show when a piece can be unbuilt.
settings.descCellSizeH: Horizontal size of the cells in the UI grid.
settings.descCellSizeY: Vertical size of the cells in the UI grid.
settings.descResourceIcon: The sprite used as an icon for resources.
settings.descResourceContainerHeight: Height of the resource container in the UI.
settings.descResourceImageWidth: Width of the resource image in the UI.
settings.descResourceImageHeight: Height of the resource image in the UI.
settings.descResourceImageAnchorMin: Minimum anchor point for the resource image.
settings.descResourceImageAnchorMax: Maximum anchor point for the resource image.
settings.descResourceImagePivot: Pivot point for the resource image.
settings.descResourceTextFontSize: Font size of the resource text.
settings.descResourceVerticalAlign: Vertical alignment offset for the resource text.
settings.descResourceTextSize: Size of the resource text box.
settings.descResourceTextAlignment: Alignment of the resource text within its box.
settings.descResourceTextAutoSizing: Whether the resource text automatically adjusts its size.
settings.descSkeletonColor: The material used to color the skeleton of the model.
settings.descSkeletonTransparentColor: The material used to make the skeleton of the model transparent.
settings.descPopupPrefab: The prefab used for creating the popup UI.
settings.descPopupButtonSprite: The sprite used for the build button in the popup.
settings.descPopupUnbuildButtonSprite: The sprite used for the unbuild button in the popup.
settings.descPopupSpritePanel: The sprite used for the popup panel background.
settings.descPopupSpriteIron: The sprite used to represent iron resources in the popup.
settings.descPopupSpritePlastic: The sprite used to represent plastic resources in the popup.
settings.descPopupSpriteRubber: The sprite used to represent rubber resources in the popup.
settings.descResourceIconSize: The size of the resource icons displayed in the popup.
settings.descResourceSpacing: The spacing between resource icons in the popup.
settings.descPopupResourceTextColor: The color of the resource text in the popup.
settings.descPopupResourceTextFontSize: The font size of the resource text in the popup.
settings.descPopupSize: The size of the popup panel.
settings.descPopupOffset: The offset of the popup panel relative to the button.
settings.descPopupPadding: The padding values (left, right, top, bottom) for the popup panel.
settings.descSpacingBetweenElements: The spacing between elements inside the popup panel.
settings.descMouseSensitivity: Controls the sensitivity of the mouse for camera rotation.
settings.descMovementSpeed: Determines the speed at which the camera moves in the scene.
settings.descBoundaryCollider: Defines the boundaries within which the camera can move.
settings.descOrbitRotationSpeed: Controls the speed of the camera's rotation around the target.
settings.descOrbitZoomSpeed: Determines the speed at which the camera zooms in and out.
settings.descOrbitMinZoom: The minimum distance the camera can zoom in towards the target.
settings.descOrbitMaxZoom: The maximum distance the camera can zoom out from the target.
settings.descOrbitTarget: The target object around which the camera orbits.
settings.descUsePerformanceMode: Toggles performance mode to optimize camera behavior.
settings.descOrbitMouseSensitivity: Adjusts the sensitivity of mouse input for camera control.
settings.descPosCameraSpeed: Controls the speed of the camera's movement during positioning.
settings.descPosRotationSpeed: Determines the speed of the camera's rotation around the target.
settings.descPosZoomSpeed: Adjusts the speed at which the camera zooms in and out.
settings.descPosMinZoom: The minimum distance the camera can zoom in towards the target.
settings.descPosMaxZoom: The maximum distance the camera can zoom out from the target.
settings.descPosTarget: The target object that the camera focuses on during positioning.
settings.descPosOffset: The offset position of the camera relative to the target.
settings.descEnableDebugLogs: Enables or disables debug logs for camera positioning actions.
settings.descPrefabPlacementSystem: Manages the placement of prefabs during camera positioning.
settings.descMainCamera: The main camera used for positioning and visualization. If not specified, Camera.main is used.
settings.descPlacementSurfaces: The layers on which objects can be placed.
settings.descConstructionUI: Reference to the construction UI for interaction.
settings.descViewDetails: Reference to the component that manages display details.
settings.descAudioSource: Audio source for sound effects during placement.
settings.descEnableCameraFollow: If enabled, the camera will follow the object during placement.
settings.descCameraOffset: Camera offset relative to the object during placement.
settings.descAllowFineMovement: Allows precise movement with WASD during placement.
settings.descRaiseObjectKey: Key to raise the object during placement.
settings.descLowerObjectKey: Key to lower the object during placement.
settings.descPlacementRotationSpeed: Object rotation speed (in degrees per second).
settings.descMaxHeightAdjustment: Maximum height adjustment limit up and down.
settings.descValidPlacementMaterial: Material applied to the prefab when placement is valid.
settings.descInvalidPlacementMaterial: Material applied to the prefab when placement is invalid.
settings.descPreviewAlpha: Transparency level of the prefab during preview (0-1).
settings.descPlacementValidSound: Sound played when placement becomes valid.
settings.descPlacementInvalidSound: Sound played when placement becomes invalid.
settings.descRotateSound: Sound played when rotating the object.
settings.descUsePlacementBoundary: If enabled, limits placement within a defined area.
settings.descUseVisualBoundary: If enabled, displays the placement area limits in the editor.
settings.descBoxSize: Dimensions of the cube that defines the placement area (width, height, depth).
settings.descBoundaryColor: Color of the placement area displayed in the editor.
settings.descUseHeightLimit: If enabled, uses custom height limits instead of cube limits.
settings.descMinHeightLimit: Minimum height allowed for placement when _useHeightLimit is active.
settings.descMaxHeightLimit: Maximum height allowed for placement when _useHeightLimit is active.
settings.constructionText: In the Hierarchy, you can find the Construction UI GameObject which contains all the settings for:
settings.constructionList3: Individual settings for each piece of each model that will be displayed in the left grid
settings.constructionList4: Settings for the popup that appears when clicking on a piece
settings.constructionNote: These settings allow you to fully customize the appearance and behavior of your interactive models.
settings.freeCamText: These settings control the free camera that activates when you press the <kbd>L</kbd> key. You can adjust:
settings.freeCamList3: The free camera boundaries are determined by the size of the box collider located in the "Collider_FreeCamera" GameObject. By resizing this collider, you can precisely set the allowed movement area for the camera.
settings.orbitCamText: These settings control the orbit camera that rotates around your model. You can configure:
settings.orbitCamPieceText: For each DescriptionComponent associated with a piece, you can modify:
settings.orbitCamPieceList1: Specific camera rotation when the piece is selected
settings.orbitCamPieceList2: Camera distance from the selected piece (automatic zoom)
settings.orbitCamPieceList3: Camera angle relative to the piece for optimal viewing
settings.performanceList1: Resource usage improvement: Using a more "raw" mode to determine camera position (such as using only the target position instead of considering a renderer's bounds), reduces complex calculations, freeing up resources for other tasks.
settings.performanceList2: Application context: Performance mode is useful in situations where the user is in a resource-intensive scene (for example, when there are many objects to load, such as in a scene with many prefabs) and a lower computational load is desired.
settings.performanceTech1: Simplified position calculation: When performance mode is activated, the position of the targetTransform is simply used as the center of the object, without calculating the renderer bounds (which requires more computational resources).
settings.performanceTech2: Reduced interpolation: With performance mode active, the interpolation speed of position and rotation is reduced through the smoothFactor multiplier, which decreases the "slowness" of camera movement to further reduce computational load.
settings.zoomVideoCaption: Demo video: automatic zoom on selected pieces
settings.effectsText: These settings relate to the sound and visual effects associated with each prefab. You can adjust:
settings.effectsNote: Customizing these settings allows you to create a more immersive and interactive experience for your users.
settings.placementSysText: These settings control the system for positioning completed prefabs in the game environment. In this section you can configure:
settings.placementSysList1: Audio effects for placement and rotation
settings.placementSysList4: Grid settings (cell size, vertical/horizontal speed, color, visualization size)
settings.placementSysNote: Properly customizing these settings is essential to ensure a smooth and intuitive placement experience. Visual and audio indicators help the user understand when a placement is valid or invalid.
settings.placementSysCustom: Custom Placement Areas: You can also define specific boundaries within the placement area by modifying the Exclusion Zone settings. This allows you to create designated regions where objects can or cannot be placed, giving you precise control over your environment layout and creating specific boundaries inside the placement area.
settings.videoFallback: Your browser does not support HTML5 video.

lodGroup.title: 11. LOD GROUP
lodGroup.supportText1: Assembly Station fully supports models that use LOD Groups (Level of Detail), allowing you to create interactive assemblies even with models optimized for different viewing distances.
lodGroup.supportText2: When working with models containing LOD Groups, remember that:
lodGroup.supportRule1Prefix: The Descriptor Component must be applied to the LOD0 level (maximum detail) of the prefab
lodGroup.supportTip: Tip: The Descriptor Component is documented in detail in the
lodGroup.supportTipEnd: section of this documentation. Refer to it for complete instructions on how to add and configure this component.
lodGroup.supportRule2: The Assembly system will automatically recognize LOD Groups and correctly handle the transition between different detail levels
lodGroup.supportRule3: All settings for LOD groups remain the same as used in the rest of the system
lodGroup.benefitsText: Using LOD Groups with Assembly Station offers significant performance advantages for complex scenes:
lodGroup.benefit1: Reduced GPU load when objects are distant
lodGroup.benefit2: Ability to use highly detailed models without compromising performance
lodGroup.benefit3: Greater application scalability across devices with different hardware capabilities
lodGroup.configText: To correctly configure a model with LOD Groups for use in Assembly Station:
lodGroup.configStep1Title: Prepare your model with different detail levels
lodGroup.configStep1Text: Ensure your model has correctly configured LOD levels (LOD0, LOD1, LOD2, etc.)
lodGroup.configStep2Text: Always add the Descriptor Components to the LOD0 level (maximum detail) of the model
lodGroup.configStep3Text: Configure all other properties as you would for any other Assembly Station component
lodGroup.configWarning: Note on LOD groups: If you want to group multiple LOD models under a single button in the user interface, refer to the instructions in section
lodGroup.configWarningEnd: of the documentation. Pay special attention not to confuse LOD group names with categorization tags, as they serve different functions. LOD groups manage detail levels, while tags in section 6 control how components are grouped in the user interface.
lodGroup.configNote: Note: Assembly Station will automatically use the appropriate detail levels based on the camera's distance from the object. No additional LOD Group-specific configuration is needed.
lodGroup.actionText: See how LOD Groups work in Assembly Station with this demonstration video. Notice how the system smoothly transitions between different detail levels based on camera distance, maintaining visual quality while optimizing performance.
lodGroup.actionCaption: LOD Group demonstration - smooth transitions between different detail levels
lodGroup.namingWarning: WARNING: The names of different LOD levels cannot be identical, otherwise the system will not function correctly. Use the following naming scheme to ensure optimal operation:
lodGroup.namingBenefitsText: This naming structure provides several benefits:
lodGroup.namingClarity: Clarity: Each LOD level has a unique name that includes both the object identifier and the LOD level
lodGroup.namingOrganization: Organization: Makes it easier to manage complex hierarchies with multiple LOD groups
lodGroup.namingConsistency: Consistency: Helps maintain a uniform structure across different models and projects
lodGroup.namingImportant: Important: While you can use any naming convention you prefer, ensure that your Descriptor Component is always attached to the LOD0 (highest detail) object in each LOD Group.
lodGroup.bestPractice1: Verify correct LOD configuration: Make sure the LOD Groups are configured with smooth transitions between levels
lodGroup.bestPractice2: Test at different distances: Check your assembly behavior at various distances to ensure LODs work as expected
lodGroup.bestPractice3: Progressively optimize: Significantly reduce polygon counts in lower LOD levels to maximize performance benefits
lodGroup.videoFallback: Your browser does not support the HTML5 video tag.

blockPiece.title: 12. PIECE LOCKING SYSTEM
blockPiece.lead: The piece locking system allows you to create a progression mechanic in your assembly process, requiring users to build a certain number of pieces before unlocking others.
blockPiece.lockedText: When a piece is locked, it will appear with a lock icon in the interface. The user must build the required number of other pieces before being able to place the locked piece. This creates a strategic progression system for your assembly projects.
blockPiece.howText: To lock a piece, follow these steps:
blockPiece.howStep1: Navigate to the Piece Descriptor of the piece you want to lock
blockPiece.howStep2: Check the Lock Piece checkbox to activate the locking system
blockPiece.howStep3: Set the Lock Level value, which determines how many pieces must be built before this piece becomes available
blockPiece.howExplanation: The lock level represents the number of pieces that must be constructed before this piece becomes available to the user. For example, if you set the lock level to 5, the user must build at least 5 other pieces before this one becomes unlocked.
blockPiece.howTip: Tip: Use this feature to guide users through a logical building sequence or to create a challenge progression in your assembly instructions.
blockPiece.groupText1: When working with grouped pieces, remember to apply the lock settings to the main piece of the group (the one checked in GroupPieces). As explained in the
blockPiece.groupText1End: , the first piece in a group controls all settings for the entire group.
blockPiece.groupText2: If you apply lock settings to secondary pieces in a group, they will be ignored since the group is controlled by its main piece.
blockPiece.strategyText: The piece locking system adds a new dimension to your assembly instructions:
blockPiece.strategy1: Guided progression: Lead users through a logical building sequence
blockPiece.strategy2: Challenge escalation: Create increasing difficulty as the build progresses
blockPiece.strategy3: Achievement system: Reward users with new pieces as they make progress
blockPiece.strategy4: Educational pacing: Ensure complex parts are only accessible after users have learned the basics

hub.title: My Unity Assets
hub.subtitle: Explore the documentation for my published Unity assets
hub.asCardDesc: Assembly Station is the ultimate tool for creating interactive assembly instructions in Unity. Designed to streamline complex assembly processes with intuitive visual feedback.
hub.hmCardDesc: A complete guided missile launcher system for Unity 6. Real Proportional Navigation guidance, military HUD with UI Toolkit, and procedural lock-on audio.

hmIntro.comingSoon: Coming Soon to Unity Asset Store
hmIntro.subtitle: Complete Guided Missile Launcher System for Unity 6
hmIntro.heroText: Homing Missile delivers everything you need for a realistic guided-missile launcher station — Proportional Navigation guidance, military-grade HUD, procedural lock-on audio, and a turret the player can control from the first frame.
hmIntro.getBtnAria: Get Homing Missile from Unity Asset Store
hmIntro.docSubtitle: Complete script reference, inspector fields, events, and step-by-step integration guide.
hmIntro.aboutP1: Homing Missile is a self-contained, ready-to-use missile launcher system for Unity 6 with URP. Drop the prefabs into your scene, assign four tags, and you have a fully functional launcher station with real missile physics and a military HUD.
hmIntro.aboutP2: Every field is exposed in the Inspector and fully documented. The included demo scenes let you explore the entire system immediately out of the box.
hmIntro.docCardText: Complete documentation for all 13 scripts and inspector fields
hmIntro.setupCardText: Quick step-by-step guide to integrate in your own scene
hmIntro.techCardText: Physics, audio architecture, and implementation details
hmIntro.ctaText: Get Homing Missile from the Unity Asset Store and start building your combat scene today.
hmIntro.demoLead: Try the Homing Missile system directly in your browser. Launch missiles, lock on targets and see the PN guidance in action.
hmIntro.disclaimerText: This demo may not reflect the latest version of the project. It is a different, non-optimized build, so you may experience visual or audio bugs. It is only meant as a quick representation of the asset.
hmIntro.demoClickText: to interact. Use mouse to aim and fire.
hmIntro.demoWebglNote: Requires a WebGL-compatible browser. Best experienced on desktop.

hmLicenses.lead: Homing Missile uses various third-party assets, tools, and resources that are distributed under different licenses. This page provides attribution and licensing information for all third-party content included in this asset.
hmLicenses.texturesAmbientCG: This asset includes plane/ground textures ("Tiles106" series: BaseColor, AmbientOcclusion, Displacement, NormalDX, NormalGL, Roughness) from AmbientCG under Creative Commons CC0 1.0 Universal.
hmLicenses.audioAdobe: This asset includes audio files (explosion.wav, missile-navigazione.wav, partenza.wav, partenza2.wav) generated using Adobe AI tools under Adobe's Terms of Use.

hmPricing.title: Pricing
hmPricing.lead: One single price, complete access: a production-ready homing missile system for your Unity project
hmPricing.feature1: Complete Homing Missile System for Unity
hmPricing.faq1Q: Is this a one-time payment or subscription?
hmPricing.faq1A: This is a one-time purchase. You'll get the current version and all future updates to the asset with no additional costs.
hmPricing.faq2Q: Can I use this asset in multiple projects?
hmPricing.faq2A: Yes, once purchased, you can use Homing Missile in multiple projects according to Unity Asset Store's standard license terms.
hmPricing.faq3Q: How do I get support if I have questions?
hmPricing.faq3A: You can contact me directly through the support channels provided in the documentation. I'm committed to helping you implement Homing Missile successfully in your projects.
hmPricing.moreInfo: Need more information before making a decision?
